perturbation.original_text:
4.  CONFIDENTIALITY

4.1 Confidentiality obligations
Unless otherwise provided in this Amendment, any Confidential Information released by either of the Parties (the "Disclosing Party") to the other Party (the "Receiving Party") shall not be released in whole or in part to any third party.
perturbation.changed_text:
4.  CONFIDENTIALITY

4.1 Confidentiality obligations
Unless otherwise provided in this Amendment, any Proprietary Information released by either of the Parties (the "Disclosing Party") to the other Party (the "Receiving Party") shall not be released in whole or in part to any third party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Confidential Information' to 'Proprietary Information' without aligning it to a legal definition applicable in New York introduces ambiguity. Confidential information has legal precedents, while proprietary information can be vague, especially regarding trade secrets under New York law.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law uses specific definitions related to trade secrets and confidential information. Replacing 'Confidential Information' with 'Proprietary Information' without a clear definition in the contract undermines the established legal protection of trade secrets under New York law, as it creates uncertainty about what information is actually protected.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 24-A General Obligations ARTICLE 5 Creation, Definition and Enforcement of Contractual Obligations TITLE 14 Enforceability of Clauses Respecting Choice of Law and Choice of Forum In Certain Transactions up TITLE 14 Enforceability of Clauses Respecting Choice of Law and Choice of Forum In Certain Transactions next SECTION 5-1402 Choice of forum This entry was published on 2018-08-31 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-1401 Choice of law General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 14 § 5-1401. Choice of law. 1. The parties to any contract, agreement or undertaking, contingent or otherwise, in consideration of, or relating to any obligation arising out of a transaction covering in the aggregate not less than two hundred fifty thousand dollars, including a transaction otherwise covered by subsection (a) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code, may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties in whole or in part, whether or not such contract, agreement or undertaking bears a reasonable relation to this state. This section shall not apply to any contract, agreement or undertaking (a) for labor or personal services, (b) relating to any transaction for personal, family or household services, or (c) to the extent provided to the contrary in subsection (c) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code. 2. Nothing contained in this section shall be construed to limit or deny the enforcement of any provision respecting choice of law in any other contract, agreement or undertaking.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Confidential Information' to 'Proprietary Information' introduces ambiguity. The Perturbation Explanation states this change undermines established legal protection, especially regarding trade secrets under New York law. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 primarily addresses 'Choice of law', the Law Explanation points out that New York law uses specific definitions for 'trade secrets and confidential information'. By using 'Proprietary Information' without a clear definition, the contract risks deviating from legally recognized terms, making it harder to enforce obligations related to confidential information and trade secrets, thus leading to potential non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text:
5.  GOVERNING LAW - ARBITRATION

5.1 Governing law: Pursuant to and in accordance with Section 5-1401 of the New York General Obligations Law, the Parties hereto agree that this Amendment in all respects, and any claim or cause of action based upon or arising out of this Amendment, or any dealing between the Parties relating to the subject matter of this Amendment or the transactions contemplated hereby or the Company/Repairer relationship being established, shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of the State of New York, U.S.A. as applied to contracts to be performed wholly within the State of New York (Exclusive of Section 7-101 of the New York General Obligations Law which is inapplicable to this Amendment).
perturbation.changed_text:
5.  GOVERNING LAW - ARBITRATION

5.1 Governing law: Pursuant to and in accordance with Section 5-1401 of the New York General Obligations Law, the Parties hereto agree that this Amendment in all respects, and any claim or cause of action based upon or arising out of this Amendment, or any dealing between the Parties relating to the subject matter of this Amendment or the transactions contemplated hereby or the Company/Repairer relationship being established, shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of the State of Delaware, U.S.A. as applied to contracts to be performed wholly within the State of New York (Exclusive of Section 7-101 of the New York General Obligations Law which is inapplicable to this Amendment).
perturbation.explanation: While referencing New York General Obligations Law, specifying Delaware law as the governing law introduces a direct conflict. This creates legal uncertainty as the laws of New York and Delaware may differ significantly in contract interpretation, remedies, and other relevant areas.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Choice of Law Principles
perturbation.law_explanation: Although NY Gen. Oblig. Law § 5-1401 allows parties to choose New York law, it does not explicitly allow choosing another state's law while still applying provisions that are specific to New York. By stating that Delaware law applies but excluding Section 7-101 of the New York General Obligations Law, the modified text creates an internal inconsistency, contradicting the intended application of New York law.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text introduces a legal contradiction by specifying Delaware law as the governing law while referencing Section 5-1401 of the New York General Obligations Law. The Perturbation Explanation clarifies that this creates legal uncertainty because the laws of New York and Delaware may differ. The Law Explanation highlights that while NY Gen. Oblig. Law § 5-1401 allows parties to choose New York law, it does not explicitly allow choosing another state's law while still applying provisions specific to New York. The changed text creates an internal inconsistency by stating that Delaware law applies but excluding Section 7-101 of the New York General Obligations Law, contradicting the intended application of New York law.

perturbation.original_text: 5.2.2 The arbitration, and any proceedings, and meetings incidental to or related to the arbitration process, shall take place in New York, U.S.A, and the language to be used in the arbitral proceedings shall be English; arbitral award shall be final and binding upon the Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.2.2 The arbitration, and any proceedings, and meetings incidental to or related to the arbitration process, shall take place in New York, U.S.A, and the language to be used in the arbitral proceedings shall be Klingon; arbitral award shall be final and binding upon the Parties.
perturbation.explanation: Specifying "Klingon" as the language for arbitration proceedings introduces absurdity and renders the arbitration clause potentially unenforceable. This deviates from standard legal practice and could be challenged as an unreasonable or bad-faith provision.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Federal Arbitration Act (FAA) aims to enforce arbitration agreements. By specifying an unusual and impractical language like Klingon, the modified clause creates an obstacle to effective arbitration, potentially undermining the agreement's enforceability under the FAA. While the FAA doesn't explicitly prohibit a made-up language, it strongly implies that the agreed-upon procedures should allow for a fair and reasonable resolution, which is not possible with a nonsensical language requirement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: U.S.C. Title 9 - ARBITRATION 9 U.S.C. United States Code, 2011 Edition Title 9 - ARBITRATION CHAPTER 1 - GENERAL PROVISIONS Sec. 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate From the U.S. Government Publishing Office, www.gpo.gov §2. Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising out of such a contract, transaction, or refusal, shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract. (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 670.) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, §2, 43 Stat. 883.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'English' to 'Klingon' as the language for arbitration creates a significant obstacle to the arbitration process. The FAA, as indicated by the provided snippet from 9 U.S.C. §2, aims to enforce arbitration agreements. Substituting a nonsensical language undermines the enforceability of the agreement because it is not a language spoken or understood by anyone involved, rendering the arbitration process impossible. This conflicts with the FAA's intent to ensure valid and enforceable arbitration agreements. The modified clause would likely fail legal scrutiny due to its impracticality.
